solution:
In order to interpret the problem statement, one must choose a convention for the value of $0^0$; we will take
it to equal 1. (If one takes $0^0$ to be 0, then the problem fails for $p=3$.)

\textbf{First solution.}
By Wilson's theorem,
\[
k! (p-1-k)! \equiv (-1)^{k} (p-1)! \equiv (-1)^{k+1} \pmod{p},
\]
so we have a congruence of Laurent polynomials
\begin{align*}
\sum_{k=0}^{p-1} k! x^k &\equiv \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} \frac{(-1)^{k+1} x^k}{(p-1-k)!} \pmod{p} \\
&\equiv -x^{p-1} \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} \frac{(-x)^{-k}}{k!} \pmod{p}.
\end{align*}
Replacing $x$ with $-1/x$, we reduce the original problem to showing that the polynomial
\[
g(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} \frac{x^k}{k!}
\]
over $\FF_p$ has at most $(p-1)/2$ nonzero roots in $\FF_p$. To see this, write
\[
h(x) = x^p - x + g(x)
\]
and note that by Wilson's theorem again,
\[
h'(x) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p-1} \frac{x^{k-1}}{(k-1)!} = x^{p-1} - 1 + g(x).
\]
If $z \in \FF_p$ is such that $g(z) = 0$, then $z \neq 0$ because $g(0) = 1$.
Therefore, $z^{p-1} = 1$, so $h(z) = h'(z) = 0$ and so $z$ is at least a double root of $h$.
Since $h$ is a polynomial of degree $p$, there can be at most $(p-1)/2$ zeroes of $g$ in $\FF_p$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.} (By Noam Elkies)
Define the polynomial $f$ over $\FF_p$ by
\[
f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} k! x^k.
\]
Put $t = (p-1)/2$; the problem statement is that $f$
has at most $t$ roots modulo $p$.
Suppose the contrary; since $f(0) = 1$,
this means that $f(x)$ is nonzero for at most $t-1$ values of $x \in \FF_p^*$.
Denote these values by $x_1,\dots,x_m$, where by assumption $m < t$, and define the polynomial
$Q$ over $\FF_p$ by
\[
Q(x) =
\prod_{k=1}^m (x - x_m) = \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} Q_k x^k.
\]
Then we can write
\[
f(x) = \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} (1-x^{p-1})
\]
where $P(x)$ is some polynomial of degree at most $m$.
This means that the power series expansions of $f(x)$ and $P(x)/Q(x)$ coincide modulo $x^{p-1}$,
so the coefficients of $x^t, \dots, x^{2t-1}$ in $f(x) Q(x)$ vanish.
In other words, the product of the square matrix
\[
A = ((i+j+1)!)_{i,j=0}^{t-1}
\]
with the nonzero column vector $(Q_{t-1}, \dots, Q_0)$ is zero.
However, by the following lemma, $\det(A)$ is nonzero modulo $p$, a contradiction.

\begin{lemma}
For any nonnegative integer $m$ and any integer $n$,
\[
\det((i+j+n)!)_{i,j=0}^m = \prod_{k=0}^m k! (k+n)!.
\]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Define the $(m+1) \times (m+1)$ matrix $A_{m,n}$ by $(A_{m,n})_{i,j} = \binom{i+j+n}{i}$;
the desired result is then that $\det(A_{m,n}) = 1$. Note that
\[
(A_{m,n-1})_{ij} = \begin{cases}
(A_{m,n})_{ij} & i=0 \\
(A_{m,n})_{ij} - (A_{m,n})_{(i-1)j} & i > 0;
\end{cases}
\]
that is, $A_{m,n-1}$ can be obtained from $A_{m,n}$ by elementary row operations.
Therefore, $\det(A_{m,n}) = \det(A_{m,n-1})$, so $\det(A_{m,n})$ depends only on $m$.
The claim now follows by observing that $A_{0,0}$ is the $1 \times 1$ matrix with entry 1
and that $A_{m,-1}$ has the block representation $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & * \\ 0 & A_{m-1,0} \end{pmatrix}$.
\end{proof}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Elkies has given a more detailed discussion of the origins of this solution in the theory of orthogonal
polynomials; see \texttt{http://mathoverflow.net/questions/82648}.


\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Let $primep$ be an odd prime. Show that for at least $(primep+1)/2$ values of $indexn$ in $\{0,1,2,\dots,primep-1\}$,
\[
\sum_{indexk=0}^{primep-1} indexk!\, indexn^{indexk} \qquad \mbox{is not divisible by $primep$.}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
In order to interpret the problem statement, one must choose a convention for the value of $0^0$; we will take
it to equal 1. (If one takes $0^0$ to be 0, then the problem fails for $primep=3$.)

\textbf{First solution.}
By Wilson's theorem,
\[
indexk!\,(primep-1-indexk)! \equiv (-1)^{indexk} (primep-1)! \equiv (-1)^{indexk+1} \pmod{primep},
\]
so we have a congruence of Laurent polynomials
\begin{align*}
\sum_{indexk=0}^{primep-1} indexk!\, \variablex^{indexk} &\equiv \sum_{indexk=0}^{primep-1} \frac{(-1)^{indexk+1}\, \variablex^{indexk}}{(primep-1-indexk)!} \pmod{primep} \\
&\equiv -\variablex^{primep-1} \sum_{indexk=0}^{primep-1} \frac{(-\variablex)^{-indexk}}{indexk!} \pmod{primep}.
\end{align*}
Replacing $\variablex$ with $-1/\variablex$, we reduce the original problem to showing that the polynomial
\[
polynomialg(\variablex) = \sum_{indexk=0}^{primep-1} \frac{\variablex^{indexk}}{indexk!}
\]
over $\FF_{primep}$ has at most $(primep-1)/2$ nonzero roots in $\FF_{primep}$. To see this, write
\[
polynomialh(\variablex) = \variablex^{primep} - \variablex + polynomialg(\variablex)
\]
and note that by Wilson's theorem again,
\[
polynomialh'(\variablex) = 1 + \sum_{indexk=1}^{primep-1} \frac{\variablex^{indexk-1}}{(indexk-1)!} = \variablex^{primep-1} - 1 + polynomialg(\variablex).
\]
If $\variablez \in \FF_{primep}$ is such that $polynomialg(\variablez) = 0$, then $\variablez \neq 0$ because $polynomialg(0) = 1$.
Therefore, $\variablez^{primep-1} = 1$, so $polynomialh(\variablez) = polynomialh'(\variablez) = 0$ and so $\variablez$ is at least a double root of $polynomialh$.
Since $polynomialh$ is a polynomial of degree $primep$, there can be at most $(primep-1)/2$ zeroes of $polynomialg$ in $\FF_{primep}$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.} (By Noam Elkies)
Define the polynomial $polynomialf$ over $\FF_{primep}$ by
\[
polynomialf(\variablex) = \sum_{indexk=0}^{primep-1} indexk!\, \variablex^{indexk}.
\]
Put $paramt = (primep-1)/2$; the problem statement is that $polynomialf$
has at most $paramt$ roots modulo $primep$.
Suppose the contrary; since $polynomialf(0) = 1$,
this means that $polynomialf(\variablex)$ is nonzero for at most $paramt-1$ values of $\variablex \in \FF_{primep}^*$.
Denote these values by $firstx,\dots,\terminalx$, where by assumption $paramm < paramt$, and define the polynomial
$polynomialq$ over $\FF_{primep}$ by
\[
polynomialq(\variablex) =
\prod_{indexk=1}^{paramm} (\variablex - \terminalx) = \sum_{indexk=0}^{paramt-1} polynomialq_{indexk}\, \variablex^{indexk}.
\]
Then we can write
\[
polynomialf(\variablex) = \frac{polynomialp(\variablex)}{polynomialq(\variablex)}\,(1-\variablex^{primep-1})
\]
where $polynomialp(\variablex)$ is some polynomial of degree at most $paramm$.
This means that the power series expansions of $polynomialf(\variablex)$ and $polynomialp(\variablex)/polynomialq(\variablex)$ coincide modulo $\variablex^{primep-1}$,
so the coefficients of $\variablex^{paramt}, \dots, \variablex^{2 paramt - 1}$ in $polynomialf(\variablex)\, polynomialq(\variablex)$ vanish.
In other words, the product of the square matrix
\[
matrixa = ((indexi+indexj+1)!)_{indexi,indexj=0}^{paramt-1}
\]
with the nonzero column vector $(polynomialq_{paramt-1}, \dots, polynomialq_0)$ is zero.
However, by the following lemma, $\det(matrixa)$ is nonzero modulo $primep$, a contradiction.

\begin{lemma}
For any nonnegative integer $paramm$ and any integer $indexn$,
\[
\det((indexi+indexj+indexn)!)_{indexi,indexj=0}^{paramm} = \prod_{indexk=0}^{paramm} indexk!\,(indexk+indexn)!.
\]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Define the $(paramm+1) \times (paramm+1)$ matrix $matrixa_{paramm,indexn}$ by $(matrixa_{paramm,indexn})_{indexi,indexj} = \binom{indexi+indexj+indexn}{indexi}$;
the desired result is then that $\det(matrixa_{paramm,indexn}) = 1$. Note that
\[
(matrixa_{paramm,indexn-1})_{indexi indexj} = \begin{cases}
(matrixa_{paramm,indexn})_{indexi indexj} & indexi=0 \\
(matrixa_{paramm,indexn})_{indexi indexj} - (matrixa_{paramm,indexn})_{(indexi-1) indexj} & indexi > 0;
\end{cases}
\]
that is, $matrixa_{paramm,indexn-1}$ can be obtained from $matrixa_{paramm,indexn}$ by elementary row operations.
Therefore, $\det(matrixa_{paramm,indexn}) = \det(matrixa_{paramm,indexn-1})$, so $\det(matrixa_{paramm,indexn})$ depends only on $paramm$.
The claim now follows by observing that $matrixa_{0,0}$ is the $1 \times 1$ matrix with entry 1
and that $matrixa_{paramm,-1}$ has the block representation $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & * \\ 0 & matrixa_{paramm-1,0} \end{pmatrix}$.
\end{proof}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Elkies has given a more detailed discussion of the origins of this solution in the theory of orthogonal
polynomials; see \texttt{http://mathoverflow.net/questions/82648}.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $strawberry$ be an odd prime. Show that for at least $(strawberry+1)/2$ values of $hinterland$ in $\{0,1,2,\dots,strawberry-1\}$,
\[
\sum_{cloudberry=0}^{strawberry-1} cloudberry!\,hinterland^{cloudberry} \qquad \mbox{is not divisible by $strawberry$.}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
In order to interpret the problem statement, one must choose a convention for the value of $0^0$; we will take it to equal 1. (If one takes $0^0$ to be 0, then the problem fails for $strawberry=3$.)

\textbf{First solution.}
By Wilson's theorem,
\[
cloudberry!\,(strawberry-1-cloudberry)! \equiv (-1)^{cloudberry}(strawberry-1)! \equiv (-1)^{cloudberry+1} \pmod{strawberry},
\]
so we have a congruence of Laurent polynomials
\begin{align*}
\sum_{cloudberry=0}^{strawberry-1} cloudberry!\,lighthouse^{cloudberry} &\equiv \sum_{cloudberry=0}^{strawberry-1} \frac{(-1)^{cloudberry+1}lighthouse^{cloudberry}}{(strawberry-1-cloudberry)!} \pmod{strawberry} \
&\equiv -lighthouse^{strawberry-1} \sum_{cloudberry=0}^{strawberry-1} \frac{(-lighthouse)^{-cloudberry}}{cloudberry!} \pmod{strawberry}.
\end{align*}
Replacing $lighthouse$ with $-1/lighthouse$, we reduce the original problem to showing that the polynomial
\[
snowflake(lighthouse)=\sum_{cloudberry=0}^{strawberry-1}\frac{lighthouse^{cloudberry}}{cloudberry!}
\]
over $\FF_{strawberry}$ has at most $(strawberry-1)/2$ nonzero roots in $\FF_{strawberry}$. To see this, write
\[
rainforest(lighthouse)=lighthouse^{strawberry}-lighthouse+snowflake(lighthouse)
\]
and note that by Wilson's theorem again,
\[
rainforest'(lighthouse)=1+\sum_{cloudberry=1}^{strawberry-1}\frac{lighthouse^{cloudberry-1}}{(cloudberry-1)!}=lighthouse^{strawberry-1}-1+snowflake(lighthouse).
\]
If $harvestmoon\in\FF_{strawberry}$ is such that $snowflake(harvestmoon)=0$, then $harvestmoon\neq0$ because $snowflake(0)=1$.
Therefore, $harvestmoon^{strawberry-1}=1$, so $rainforest(harvestmoon)=rainforest'(harvestmoon)=0$ and so $harvestmoon$ is at least a double root of $rainforest$.
Since $rainforest$ is a polynomial of degree $strawberry$, there can be at most $(strawberry-1)/2$ zeroes of $snowflake$ in $\FF_{strawberry}$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.} (By Noam Elkies)
Define the polynomial $umbrella$ over $\FF_{strawberry}$ by
\[
umbrella(lighthouse)=\sum_{cloudberry=0}^{strawberry-1} cloudberry!\,lighthouse^{cloudberry}.
\]
Put $milkshakes=(strawberry-1)/2$; the problem statement is that $umbrella$ has at most $milkshakes$ roots modulo $strawberry$.
Suppose the contrary; since $umbrella(0)=1$, this means that $umbrella(lighthouse)$ is nonzero for at most $milkshakes-1$ values of $lighthouse\in\FF_{strawberry}^*$.
Denote these values by $lighthouseone,\dots,lighthousemany$, where by assumption $pineapple<milkshakes$, and define the polynomial $dandelion$ over $\FF_{strawberry}$ by
\[
dandelion(lighthouse)=\prod_{cloudberry=1}^{pineapple}(lighthouse-lighthousemany)=\sum_{cloudberry=0}^{milkshakes-1} dandelion_{cloudberry}lighthouse^{cloudberry}.
\]
Then we can write
\[
umbrella(lighthouse)=\frac{caterpillar(lighthouse)}{dandelion(lighthouse)}(1-lighthouse^{strawberry-1})
\]
where $caterpillar(lighthouse)$ is some polynomial of degree at most $pineapple$.
This means that the power series expansions of $umbrella(lighthouse)$ and $caterpillar(lighthouse)/dandelion(lighthouse)$ coincide modulo $lighthouse^{strawberry-1}$, so the coefficients of $lighthouse^{milkshakes},\dots,lighthouse^{2milkshakes-1}$ in $umbrella(lighthouse)dandelion(lighthouse)$ vanish.
In other words, the product of the square matrix
\[
butterbell=((i+riverbank+1)!)_{i,riverbank=0}^{milkshakes-1}
\]
with the nonzero column vector $(dandelion_{milkshakes-1},\dots,dandelion_0)$ is zero.
However, by the following lemma, $\det(butterbell)$ is nonzero modulo $strawberry$, a contradiction.

\begin{lemma}
For any nonnegative integer $pineapple$ and any integer $hinterland$,
\[
\det((i+riverbank+hinterland)!)_{i,riverbank=0}^{pineapple}=\prod_{cloudberry=0}^{pineapple} cloudberry!(cloudberry+hinterland)!.
\]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Define the $(pineapple+1)\times(pineapple+1)$ matrix $A_{pineapple,hinterland}$ by $(A_{pineapple,hinterland})_{i,riverbank}=\binom{i+riverbank+hinterland}{i}$; the desired result is then that $\det(A_{pineapple,hinterland})=1$. Note that
\[
(A_{pineapple,hinterland-1})_{i riverbank}=\begin{cases}(A_{pineapple,hinterland})_{i riverbank}& i=0\\(A_{pineapple,hinterland})_{i riverbank}-(A_{pineapple,hinterland})_{(i-1) riverbank}& i>0;\end{cases}
\]
that is, $A_{pineapple,hinterland-1}$ can be obtained from $A_{pineapple,hinterland}$ by elementary row operations.
Therefore, $\det(A_{pineapple,hinterland})=\det(A_{pineapple,hinterland-1})$, so $\det(A_{pineapple,hinterland})$ depends only on $pineapple$.
The claim now follows by observing that $A_{0,0}$ is the $1\times1$ matrix with entry 1 and that $A_{pineapple,-1}$ has the block representation $\begin{pmatrix}1 & *\\0 & A_{pineapple-1,0}\end{pmatrix}$.
\end{proof}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Elkies has given a more detailed discussion of the origins of this solution in the theory of orthogonal polynomials; see \texttt{http://mathoverflow.net/questions/82648}.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $composite$ be an odd prime. Show that for at least $(composite+1)/2$ values of $floatparam$ in $\{0,1,2,\dots,composite-1\}$,
\[
\sum_{limitvalue=0}^{composite-1} limitvalue!\, floatparam^{limitvalue} \qquad \mbox{is not divisible by $composite$.}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
In order to interpret the problem statement, one must choose a convention for the value of $0^0$; we will take
it to equal 1. (If one takes $0^0$ to be 0, then the problem fails for $composite=3$.)

\textbf{First solution.}
By Wilson's theorem,
\[
limitvalue! (composite-1-limitvalue)! \equiv (-1)^{limitvalue} (composite-1)! \equiv (-1)^{limitvalue+1} \pmod{composite},
\]
so we have a congruence of Laurent polynomials
\begin{align*}
\sum_{limitvalue=0}^{composite-1} limitvalue!\, steadyconst^{limitvalue} &\equiv \sum_{limitvalue=0}^{composite-1} \frac{(-1)^{limitvalue+1} steadyconst^{limitvalue}}{(composite-1-limitvalue)!} \pmod{composite} \\
&\equiv -steadyconst^{composite-1} \sum_{limitvalue=0}^{composite-1} \frac{(-steadyconst)^{-limitvalue}}{limitvalue!} \pmod{composite}.
\end{align*}
Replacing $steadyconst$ with $-1/steadyconst$, we reduce the original problem to showing that the polynomial
\[
staticfunc(steadyconst) = \sum_{limitvalue=0}^{composite-1} \frac{steadyconst^{limitvalue}}{limitvalue!}
\]
over $\FF_{composite}$ has at most $(composite-1)/2$ nonzero roots in $\FF_{composite}$. To see this, write
\[
restpoly(steadyconst) = steadyconst^{composite} - steadyconst + staticfunc(steadyconst)
\]
and note that by Wilson's theorem again,
\[
restpoly'(steadyconst) = 1 + \sum_{limitvalue=1}^{composite-1} \frac{steadyconst^{limitvalue-1}}{(limitvalue-1)!} = steadyconst^{composite-1} - 1 + staticfunc(steadyconst).
\]
If $unboundvar \in \FF_{composite}$ is such that $staticfunc(unboundvar) = 0$, then $unboundvar \neq 0$ because $staticfunc(0) = 1$.
Therefore, $unboundvar^{composite-1} = 1$, so $restpoly(unboundvar) = restpoly'(unboundvar) = 0$ and so $unboundvar$ is at least a double root of $restpoly$.
Since $restpoly$ is a polynomial of degree $composite$, there can be at most $(composite-1)/2$ zeroes of $staticfunc$ in $\FF_{composite}$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.} (By Noam Elkies)
Define the polynomial $antipoly$ over $\FF_{composite}$ by
\[
antipoly(steadyconst) = \sum_{limitvalue=0}^{composite-1} limitvalue!\, steadyconst^{limitvalue}.
\]
Put $doubleval = (composite-1)/2$; the problem statement is that $antipoly$
has at most $doubleval$ roots modulo $composite$.
Suppose the contrary; since $antipoly(0) = 1$,
this means that $antipoly(steadyconst)$ is nonzero for at most $doubleval-1$ values of $steadyconst \in \FF_{composite}^*$.
Denote these values by $finalplaceone,\dots,originplacem$, where by assumption $limitless < doubleval$, and define the polynomial
$randompoly$ over $\FF_{composite}$ by
\[
randompoly(steadyconst) =
\prod_{limitvalue=1}^{limitless} (steadyconst - originplacem) = \sum_{limitvalue=0}^{doubleval-1} randompoly_{limitvalue} \, steadyconst^{limitvalue}.
\]
Then we can write
\[
antipoly(steadyconst) = \frac{reciprofunc(steadyconst)}{randompoly(steadyconst)} (1-steadyconst^{composite-1})
\]
where $reciprofunc(steadyconst)$ is some polynomial of degree at most $limitless$.
This means that the power series expansions of $antipoly(steadyconst)$ and $reciprofunc(steadyconst)/randompoly(steadyconst)$ coincide modulo $steadyconst^{composite-1}$,
so the coefficients of $steadyconst^{doubleval}, \dots, steadyconst^{2doubleval-1}$ in $antipoly(steadyconst)\, randompoly(steadyconst)$ vanish.
In other words, the product of the square matrix
\[
scalarvar = ((endpointvar+outsetvar+1)!)_{endpointvar,outsetvar=0}^{doubleval-1}
\]
with the nonzero column vector $(randompoly_{doubleval-1}, \dots, randompoly_0)$ is zero.
However, by the following lemma, $\det(scalarvar)$ is nonzero modulo $composite$, a contradiction.

\begin{lemma}
For any nonnegative integer limitless and any integer floatparam,
\[
\det((endpointvar+outsetvar+floatparam)!)_{endpointvar,outsetvar=0}^{limitless} = \prod_{limitvalue=0}^{limitless} limitvalue!\,(limitvalue+floatparam)!.
\]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Define the $(limitless+1) \times (limitless+1)$ matrix $scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam}$ by $(scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam})_{endpointvar,outsetvar} = \binom{endpointvar+outsetvar+floatparam}{endpointvar}$;
the desired result is then that $\det(scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam}) = 1$. Note that
\[
(scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam-1})_{endpointvar,outsetvar} = \begin{cases}
(scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam})_{endpointvar,outsetvar} & endpointvar=0 \\
(scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam})_{endpointvar,outsetvar} - (scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam})_{endpointvar-1,outsetvar} & endpointvar > 0;
\end{cases}
\]
that is, $scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam-1}$ can be obtained from $scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam}$ by elementary row operations.
Therefore, $\det(scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam}) = \det(scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam-1})$, so $\det(scalarvar_{limitless,floatparam})$ depends only on $limitless$.
The claim now follows by observing that $scalarvar_{0,0}$ is the $1 \times 1$ matrix with entry 1
and that $scalarvar_{limitless,-1}$ has the block representation $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & * \\ 0 & scalarvar_{limitless-1,0} \end{pmatrix}$.
\end{proof}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Elkies has given a more detailed discussion of the origins of this solution in the theory of orthogonal
polynomials; see \texttt{http://mathoverflow.net/questions/82648}.

garbled_string.question:
Let $lkjhgfds$ be an odd prime. Show that for at least $(lkjhgfds+1)/2$ values of $vpdshfge$ in $\{0,1,2,\dots,lkjhgfds-1\}$,
\[
\sum_{mnlqwert=0}^{lkjhgfds-1} mnlqwert! vpdshfge^{mnlqwert} \qquad \mbox{is not divisible by $lkjhgfds$.}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
In order to interpret the problem statement, one must choose a convention for the value of $0^0$; we will take
it to equal 1. (If one takes $0^0$ to be 0, then the problem fails for $lkjhgfds=3$.)

\textbf{First solution.}
By Wilson's theorem,
\[
mnlqwert! (lkjhgfds-1-mnlqwert)! \equiv (-1)^{mnlqwert} (lkjhgfds-1)! \equiv (-1)^{mnlqwert+1} \pmod{lkjhgfds},
\]
so we have a congruence of Laurent polynomials
\begin{align*}
\sum_{mnlqwert=0}^{lkjhgfds-1} mnlqwert! lkrtyuio^{mnlqwert} &\equiv \sum_{mnlqwert=0}^{lkjhgfds-1} \frac{(-1)^{mnlqwert+1} lkrtyuio^{mnlqwert}}{(lkjhgfds-1-mnlqwert)!} \pmod{lkjhgfds} \\
&\equiv -lkrtyuio^{lkjhgfds-1} \sum_{mnlqwert=0}^{lkjhgfds-1} \frac{(-lkrtyuio)^{-mnlqwert}}{mnlqwert!} \pmod{lkjhgfds}.
\end{align*}
Replacing $lkrtyuio$ with $-1/lkrtyuio$, we reduce the original problem to showing that the polynomial
\[
mnbvcxsa(lkrtyuio) = \sum_{mnlqwert=0}^{lkjhgfds-1} \frac{lkrtyuio^{mnlqwert}}{mnlqwert!}
\]
over $\FF_{lkjhgfds}$ has at most $(lkjhgfds-1)/2$ nonzero roots in $\FF_{lkjhgfds}$. To see this, write
\[
qazwsxed(lkrtyuio) = lkrtyuio^{lkjhgfds} - lkrtyuio + mnbvcxsa(lkrtyuio)
\]
and note that by Wilson's theorem again,
\[
qazwsxed'(lkrtyuio) = 1 + \sum_{mnlqwert=1}^{lkjhgfds-1} \frac{lkrtyuio^{mnlqwert-1}}{(mnlqwert-1)!} = lkrtyuio^{lkjhgfds-1} - 1 + mnbvcxsa(lkrtyuio).
\]
If $plmoknij \in \FF_{lkjhgfds}$ is such that $mnbvcxsa(plmoknij) = 0$, then $plmoknij \neq 0$ because $mnbvcxsa(0) = 1$.
Therefore, $plmoknij^{lkjhgfds-1} = 1$, so $qazwsxed(plmoknij) = qazwsxed'(plmoknij) = 0$ and so $plmoknij$ is at least a double root of $qazwsxed$.
Since $qazwsxed$ is a polynomial of degree $lkjhgfds$, there can be at most $(lkjhgfds-1)/2$ zeroes of $mnbvcxsa$ in $\FF_{lkjhgfds}$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.} (By Noam Elkies)
Define the polynomial $poiulkjh$ over $\FF_{lkjhgfds}$ by
\[
poiulkjh(lkrtyuio) = \sum_{mnlqwert=0}^{lkjhgfds-1} mnlqwert! lkrtyuio^{mnlqwert}.
\]
Put $wsxqazpl = (lkjhgfds-1)/2$; the problem statement is that $poiulkjh$
has at most $wsxqazpl$ roots modulo $lkjhgfds$.
Suppose the contrary; since $poiulkjh(0) = 1$,
this means that $poiulkjh(lkrtyuio)$ is nonzero for at most $wsxqazpl-1$ values of $lkrtyuio \in \FF_{lkjhgfds}^*$.
Denote these values by $tghasdfa,\dots,bvcxzwer$, where by assumption $edcrfvtg < wsxqazpl$, and define the polynomial
zxcvbnml over $\FF_{lkjhgfds}$ by
\[
zxcvbnml(lkrtyuio) =
\prod_{mnlqwert=1}^{edcrfvtg} (lkrtyuio - bvcxzwer) = \sum_{mnlqwert=0}^{wsxqazpl-1} zxcvbnml_{mnlqwert} lkrtyuio^{mnlqwert}.
\]
Then we can write
\[
poiulkjh(lkrtyuio) = \frac{qwertyui(lkrtyuio)}{zxcvbnml(lkrtyuio)} (1-lkrtyuio^{lkjhgfds-1})
\]
where $qwertyui(lkrtyuio)$ is some polynomial of degree at most $edcrfvtg$.
This means that the power series expansions of $poiulkjh(lkrtyuio)$ and $qwertyui(lkrtyuio)/zxcvbnml(lkrtyuio)$ coincide modulo $lkrtyuio^{lkjhgfds-1}$,
so the coefficients of $lkrtyuio^{wsxqazpl}, \dots, lkrtyuio^{2\,wsxqazpl-1}$ in $poiulkjh(lkrtyuio) zxcvbnml(lkrtyuio)$ vanish.
In other words, the product of the square matrix
\[
asdfghjk = ((qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+1)!)_{qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla=0}^{wsxqazpl-1}
\]
with the nonzero column vector $(zxcvbnml_{wsxqazpl-1}, \dots, zxcvbnml_0)$ is zero.
However, by the following lemma, $\det(asdfghjk)$ is nonzero modulo $lkjhgfds$, a contradiction.

\begin{lemma}
For any nonnegative integer $edcrfvtg$ and any integer $vpdshfge$,
\[
\det((qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+vpdshfge)!)_{qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla=0}^{edcrfvtg} = \prod_{mnlqwert=0}^{edcrfvtg} mnlqwert! (mnlqwert+vpdshfge)!.
\]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Define the $(edcrfvtg+1) \times (edcrfvtg+1)$ matrix $asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge}$ by $(asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge})_{qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla} = \binom{qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+vpdshfge}{qzxwvtnp}$;
the desired result is then that $\det(asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge}) = 1$. Note that
\[
(asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge-1})_{qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla} = \begin{cases}
(asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge})_{qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla} & qzxwvtnp=0 \\
(asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge})_{qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla} - (asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge})_{(qzxwvtnp-1),hjgrksla} & qzxwvtnp > 0;
\end{cases}
\]
that is, $asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge-1}$ can be obtained from $asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge}$ by elementary row operations.
Therefore, $\det(asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge}) = \det(asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge-1})$, so $\det(asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,vpdshfge})$ depends only on $edcrfvtg$.
The claim now follows by observing that $asdfghjk_{0,0}$ is the $1 \times 1$ matrix with entry 1
and that $asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg,-1}$ has the block representation $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & * \\ 0 & asdfghjk_{edcrfvtg-1,0} \end{pmatrix}$.
\end{proof}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Elkies has given a more detailed discussion of the origins of this solution in the theory of orthogonal
polynomials; see \texttt{http://mathoverflow.net/questions/82648}.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $p$ be an odd prime.  Prove that for at least $(p-1)/2$ distinct residues
\[
S(n)= (p-1)!\,\sum_{k=0}^{p-1} k!\,n^{k},\qquad n\in\{1,2,\dots ,p-1\},
\]
 is 
not divisible by $p$.
kernel_variant.solution:
We work throughout modulo p, an odd prime.  Since Wilson's theorem says (p-1)!\equiv -1 (mod p), the factor (p-1)! in S(n) can never be zero modulo p, so it suffices to show that the polynomial

   f(x)=\sum _{k=0}^{p-1} k!\cdot x^k  in F_p[x]

has at most (p-1)/2 roots among the nonzero residues x\in F_p^\times .

1.  (Laurent-polynomial symmetry)
By Wilson's theorem,
   k!\cdot (p-1-k)! \equiv  (-1)^{k+1}  (mod p),
so for 0\leq k\leq p-1 we have
   k! \equiv  (-1)^{k+1}/(p-1-k)!  (mod p).
Hence
   f(x)\equiv  \sum _{k=0}^{p-1} (-1)^{k+1} x^k/(p-1-k)!
      = -\sum _{j=0}^{p-1} (-1)^j x^{p-1-j}/j!
      = -x^{p-1}\cdot \sum _{j=0}^{p-1} (-1)^j x^{-j}/j!
      = -x^{p-1}\cdot g(-1/x),
where we set
   g(x)=\sum _{j=0}^{p-1} x^j/j! \in  F_p[x].
It follows that for every nonzero n\in F_p^\times ,
   f(n)=0  \Leftrightarrow   g(-1/n)=0.
Thus f and g have the same number of nonzero roots in F_p^\times .

2.  (Bounding the roots of g)
Define the degree-p polynomial
   h(x)=x^p - x + g(x)
over F_p.  Its formal derivative is
   h'(x)=p\cdot x^{p-1} - 1 + g'(x) \equiv  -1 + g'(x)   (mod p).
But
   g'(x)=\sum _{k=1}^{p-1} k\cdot x^{k-1}/k! = \sum _{m=0}^{p-2} x^m/m!.
Since g(x)=\sum _{m=0}^{p-1} x^m/m!, we have
   \sum _{m=0}^{p-2} x^m/m! \equiv  g(x) - x^{p-1}/(p-1)! 
and (p-1)!\equiv -1, so
   g'(x) \equiv  g(x) + x^{p-1}  (mod p).
Hence
   h'(x) \equiv  -1 + g(x) + x^{p-1} = x^{p-1} - 1 + g(x).

Now let z\in F_p^\times  satisfy g(z)=0.  By Fermat's little theorem z^{p-1}\equiv 1, so
   h(z)=z^p-z+g(z)=z\cdot (z^{p-1}-1)+0=0,
   h'(z)=z^{p-1}-1+g(z)=1-1+0=0.
Thus z is a double root of the degree-p polynomial h.  Since a degree-p polynomial over a field can have at most p/2 distinct double roots, there are at most (p-1)/2 such z.
Therefore g has at most (p-1)/2 roots in F_p^\times , and so f has at most (p-1)/2 roots in F_p^\times .

3.  (Conclusion)
Hence among the p-1 nonzero values n\in {1,2,\ldots ,p-1}, f(n)\neq 0 for at least (p-1)/2 of them.  Multiplying back by the nonzero factor (p-1)! shows S(n)\neq 0 (mod p) for at least (p-1)/2 choices of n, as required.

core_steps: With this identity, substitute x↦−1/x and reduce the original series to g(x)=∑_{k=0}^{p−1} x^k / k! (mod p).
If g(z)=0 then z≠0 and z^{p−1}=1, so h(z)=h'(z)=0; thus every root of g is a double root of h.
Because h is degree p, g has ≤(p−1)/2 roots, giving ≥(p+1)/2 non-zero values of the original sum.

slot1.description: Whether the value n=0 is included among the tested residues (0 never contributes a root anyway).

slot2.description: A global non-zero scalar factor multiplying the whole series ∑ k! n^k; scaling does not affect divisibility.
